coredata.dc:title: Lipid droplet-targeting carbonized polymer dots for intracellular polarity visualization under wash-free conditions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Dongmei
Yao, Genxiu
Sun, Junyong
Liang, Juanjuan
Zhou, Huan
coredata.dc:description:
Background For an extended period, lipid droplets (LDs) have been regarded to be the storage depots for metabolic lipids. As a key to energy storage and regulation, lipid droplets (LDs) have been found to perform crucial functions in various physiological processes such as oxidative stress and cell migration. Abnormal polarity variations of LDs are associated with various pathological conditions, including lipid metabolism disorders, the development of atherosclerosis and cancer. Therefore, developing LD-targeting polarity-sensitive probes and effectively monitoring the variations in the LDs microenvironment is highly meaningful.
Results A highly specific LD-targeting carbonized polymer dot (LD-CPD)-based fluorescent probe with amphiphilicity was designed and synthesized under mild conditions. The new probe exhibits high polarity-sensitivity, low viscosity crosstalk, strong solvatochromic and high photobleaching resistance. The solvatochromic responses of LD-CPD are attributed to hydrogen bonding and solvent dipole-dipole interactions, with hydrogen bonding exerting a stronger influence. In addition, it demonstrates a turn-off response to water, which makes it effective for LDs washing-free imaging. Moreover, as a solvatochromic probe, the proposed CPD can well achieve ratiometric imaging of subtle polarity changes within LDs during starvation and oxidative stress, indicating its capability to observe physiological dynamic changes.
Significance All the features remind that the developed LD-CPD probe has great potential for diagnosis in LDs monitoring and analysis. It will serve as a robust monitoring platform for identifying diseases associated with abnormal LDs polarity. It also expands the application of CPDs in bioanalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer carbon dots
Polarity of LDs
Wash-free fluorescence imaging
Oxidative stress

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Starvation stimulation and oxidative stress experiment
Cell colocalization and wash free imaging experiment
Polarity changes in cells during starvation and oxidative stress

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 22304131), the Key Project of Anhui Provincial Department of Education Scientific Research Project (2023AH051750, 2023AH051749), Young Backbone Teacher In-Country Study and Research Project of Anhui Educational Committee(JNFX2023034) and Program for excellent Sci-tech Innovation Teams of Universities in Anhui Province (2023AH010073)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characteristics of LD-CPD: (A) TEM and HETEM images of LD-CPD; (B) Particle size distribution histograms of LD-CPD; (C) XRD spectra of LD-CPD; (D) XPS spectra of LD-CPD; And (E) C1s High-resolution XPS spectra of LD-CPD; (F) FTIR spectra of LD-CPD.

Spectral characteristics of LD-CPD: (A) Images of LD-CPD in different solvents under 365 nm UV lamp illumination; (B) UV–visible absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation (EX) and emission (EM) spectra of LD-CPD in DMSO; (C) Normalized emission spectra of LD-CPD in various solvents; (D) The linear relationship between the maximum emission (λem max) of LD-CPD in different solvents and the solvent polarity parameter (ET(30)); (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of LD-CPD in oil (octanol) and in water. (Illustration: images under ultraviolet light); (F) Normalized fluorescence spectra of LD-CPD in 1, 4-dioxane/aqueous solution with different water contents (0–50 %); (G) Normalized fluorescence spectra of LD-CPD in various alcohols (MeOH, EtOH, IPA, 1-Butanol, 1-Octanal).

Confocal images of LD-CPD (50 μg mL−1) in HeLa cells co-stained with Nile Red (1 μM), Lyso-Tracker Red (65 nm) and Mito-Tracker Red (100 nm) for 10 min, 30 min and 30 min, respectively. λex = 405 nm for LD-CPD; λex = 552 nm for Nile Red, Lyso-Tracker Red and Mito-Tracker Red. Scale bar = 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Confocal images of LD-CPD-stained HeLa cells incubated in nutrient-deficient conditions. The control group was cultured with serum. The ratio image corresponds to the ratio of the two-channel intensity (I Green/I Blue). Intracellular fluorescence imaging was performed with blue channel (430–495 nm) and green channel (505–570 nm) excited at 405 nm under confocal laser microscopy. Scale bar = 20 μm. (B) The mean fluorescence intensities of the cells. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Confocal images of LD-CPD-stained HeLa cells incubated in 2 mM H2O2 for 1 h. HeLa cells untreated with H2O2 were used as a control. The ratio image corresponds to the ratio of the two-channel intensity (I Green/I Blue). Intracellular fluorescence imaging was performed with blue channel (430–495 nm) and green channel (505–570 nm) excited at 405 nm under confocal laser microscopy. Scale bar = 25 μm. (B) The mean fluorescence intensities of the cells. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Synthesis of LD-CPD and its application of monitoring intracellular polarity fluctuations.

head.ce:title: Lipid droplet-targeting carbonized polymer dots for intracellular polarity visualization under wash-free conditions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Laboratory Medicine, Wannan Medical College, Wuhu, 241000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Laboratory Medicine, Wannan Medical College, Wuhu, 241000, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu, 241000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu 241000, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For an extended period, lipid droplets (LDs) have been regarded to be the storage depots for metabolic lipids. As a key to energy storage and regulation, lipid droplets (LDs) have been found to perform crucial functions in various physiological processes such as oxidative stress and cell migration. Abnormal polarity variations of LDs are associated with various pathological conditions, including lipid metabolism disorders, the development of atherosclerosis and cancer. Therefore, developing LD-targeting polarity-sensitive probes and effectively monitoring the variations in the LDs microenvironment is highly meaningful.

A highly specific LD-targeting carbonized polymer dot (LD-CPD)-based fluorescent probe with amphiphilicity was designed and synthesized under mild conditions. The new probe exhibits high polarity-sensitivity, low viscosity crosstalk, strong solvatochromic and high photobleaching resistance. The solvatochromic responses of LD-CPD are attributed to hydrogen bonding and solvent dipole-dipole interactions, with hydrogen bonding exerting a stronger influence. In addition, it demonstrates a turn-off response to water, which makes it effective for LDs washing-free imaging. Moreover, as a solvatochromic probe, the proposed CPD can well achieve ratiometric imaging of subtle polarity changes within LDs during starvation and oxidative stress, indicating its capability to observe physiological dynamic changes.

All the features remind that the developed LD-CPD probe has great potential for diagnosis in LDs monitoring and analysis. It will serve as a robust monitoring platform for identifying diseases associated with abnormal LDs polarity. It also expands the application of CPDs in bioanalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lipid droplets (LDs), recognized as the reservoirs of triacylglycerol and sterol esters, are critical lipid organelles involved in lipid storage and supply [1,2]. LDs function in various physiological processes (such as membrane transport, protein degradation and signal transduction) [3–5]. The abnormal expression of LDs is associated with many diseases, such as fatty liver [6], diabetes [7] and inflammation [8] and so on. Thus, tracking LDs is of great significance which has been a high-importance topic in recent years. The polarity of LDs is an important microenvironment in organisms that has been proven to be associated with a variety of disease processes, such as inflammation and tumorigenesis [9,10]. Therefore, noninvasive, real-time, in-situ tracking of the polarity changes of LDs is a very meaningful work for the early diagnosis of LDs polarity-related diseases.

By virtue of real-time acquisition, non-invasive, and in-situ observability, fluorescence imaging technology has been widely applied in tracking physiological processes [11–14]. At present, many polarity-sensitive LD-targeted fluorescent probes have been proposed [15,16] which are mainly organic small-molecule probes. However, small stokes shift, poor photostability and photobleaching hinder their application in living organisms. In addition, the majority intensity-dependent fluorescence probes are easy to be affected by the experimental conditions (e.g. laser intensity, inhomogeneous labeling, probe concentration), which are inconducive to LD detection. What's more, due to the nonspecific staining of other organelles, most probes suffer from unsatisfied specificity, resulting in a high background and low imaging contrast [17,18]. To overcome these shortcomings, various novel fluorescence probes have been developed [19,20]. Among them, those probes with nonemissive nature in water can facilitate experimental operations and allow for wash-free fluorescence imaging. In 2021, Prof. Li and co-workers successfully constructed a coumarin-based probe for wash-free visualization of lipid droplets, which was applied to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells [21]. Then, in 2024, Shen and colleagues reported a donor-acceptor-donor (D-A-D) probe for wash-free monitoring of lipid droplet dynamics in dry eye disease [22]. Despite the progress, there are still challenges in their bioimaging applications, such as complicated synthesis processes and high viscosity crosstalk, which lead to incomplete and inaccurate LDs imaging.

In this work, a novel nanoprobe based on carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) has been constructed for LD-specific imaging. As an emerging fluorescent nanomaterial, CPDs not only own the merits of carbon dots in improved photostability, good biocompatibility, and simple synthesis, but also exhibit strong photobleaching resistance and good intracellular retention ability [23,24]which are ideal material for organelle-targeting fluorescence imaging. However, to our knowledge, LD-targeting sensors based on carbonized polymer dots have been rarely reported [25–27]. Here, as shown in Scheme 1 , using 1, 6-dihydroxy naphthalene (DHN) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as precursors, the novel CPD, defined as LD-CPD, was designed and prepared under mild conditions which retains the lipophilicity (benzene ring) of 1, 6-dihydroxy naphthalene and hydrophilicity (amino group) of ethylenediamine, possessing appropriate amphiphilicity for LDs-specific targeting which is proven by a high Pearson's correlation coefficient with Nile Red [26]. LD-CPD exhibits significant solvatochromic emission and high sensitivity to polarity variations of LDs, showing a significant color change when decreasing the polarity of solvents. Moreover, it demonstrates a turn-off response to water and a bright green fluorescence in oily media. As expected, LD-CPD could be used for wash-free LDs imaging and successfully monitored fluctuations in intracellular polarity during starvation and oxidative stress.

1, 6-dihydroxy naphthalene was obtained from Adamas Regent, Inc. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). Organic reagents, including methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetonitrile (ACN), acetone (ACE), 1, 4-dioxane (DiOX), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), were purchased from China Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). RPMI-1640 medium was provided by Thermo Fisher Technology Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). Nile Red fluorescent probe was purchased from Maokang Biotechnology Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). Lyso-Tracker Red fluorescence probe and Mito-Tracker Red fluorescence probe were purchased from Shanghai Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. MTT cell proliferation and cytotoxicity assay kit were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). All solutions were prepared with Deionized water (≥18.25 MΩ).

Fluorescence spectra experiments were performed using Hitachi F-4700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan). The absorption spectrum in the ultraviolet–visible range was obtained using a Hitachi U-3900 spectrophotometer from Hitachi Co. Ltd, Japan. ZSE nanoparticle potential Analyzer (Malvern, Britain) was used to measure the particle size. Images of LD-CPD captured using Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were obtained with a Hitachi HT7700 instrument. High-resolution transmission images (HRTEM) were acquired by Tecnai G2 20 S-TWINX (US FEI). X-ray powder diffraction spectroscopy (XRD) was collected by SmartLab X-ray powder diffractometer (Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (400-4000 cm−1) was obtained with Bruker INVENIOS Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Germany). The absorbance of MTT assays was obtained by a Multiskan Sky microplate reader (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Confocal fluorescence images were captured on a confocal laser scanning microscope (TCS SP8, Leica, Germany). False-color images and co-localization analysis results were provided by Fiji image processing software (ImageJ).

Firstly, 0.3203 g of 1, 6-dihydroxy naphthalene was dissolved in 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Then, 3 mL ethylenediamine was added and the mixture was heated at reflux in an oil bath at 50 °C for 5 h. To remove the unreacted precursors, the reaction solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 DA) for 3 days. The black precipitates obtained by dialysis were freeze-dried. Finally, the product was dissolved in DMSO solvent to prepare 2.5 mg mL−1 black solution and stored at −4 °C for further use.

HeLa cells were cultured in RPMI-1640 medium containing 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % double antibody (penicillin-streptomycin) at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere. For assessing cytotoxicity, cells were first seeded in 96-well plates (approximately 1 × 10 4 cells per well) and cultured for 24 h. Next, they were treated with various concentrations of LD-CPD (0–100 μg mL−1) for another 24 h or 48 h. Finally, the fresh MTT reagent (10 μL) and cytolytic solution (100 μL) were added to the 96-well plates successively and cultured for 4 h, respectively. The absorbance at 570 nm was determined with an enzyme-labeled meter.

The incubation time of LD-CPD in cells was first explored. The adherent HeLa cells were incubated with a medium containing LD-CPD (40 μg mL−1) for 0 min, 5 min, 10 min, 20 min, 30 min, 40 min, 50 min, 60 min, 90 min and 120 min, respectively. Then the cells were washed with PBS before imaging. Similarly, the incubation concentration of LD-CPD was optimized. Different concentrations of LD-CPD (5 μg mL−1, 10 μg mL−1, 20 μg mL−1, 30 μg mL1, 40 μg mL−1, 50 μg mL−1, 60 μg mL−1, 80 μg mL−1) treated with HeLa cells for 50 min before imaging in PBS under a confocal microscope. The fluorescence image from LD-CPD was obtained using excitation at 405 nm, and collecting the emission in the green channel range of 480–550 nm.
HeLa cells were incubated with LD-CPD (50 μg mL−1) for 50 min. As a control to assess the performance of wash-free imaging, a subset of cells was washed twice before imaging.

The cells were preincubated in a cell incubator before the cell confluence reached approximately 80 %. After washed with PBS, they were co-incubated with LD-CPD (50 μg mL−1) for 50 min. Afterward, the cells were washed with PBS and treated with 1 mL of medium containing Nile Red (1 μM), Lyso-Tracker Red (65 nM) and Mito-Tracker Red (100 nM) for 10 min, 30 min and 30 min, respectively. Confocal imaging was then performed in PBS. The emissions of three commercial fluorescent probes were collected in the ranges of 600–650 nm (Nile Red) and 580–650 nm (Lyso-Tracker Red and Mito-Tracker Red), respectively, with the excitation at 552 nm. The Pearson correlation coefficient was calculated using ImageJ software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Starvation stimulation and oxidative stress experiment
ce:section.ce:para: During starvation treatment, cells were incubated under serum-free conditions for 3 or 7 h, while the control group was cultured with serum. Oxidative stress was induced by treating HeLa cells with a medium containing 2 mM H2O2 for 1 h. HeLa cells untreated with H2O2 were used as the control group. After treatment, the cells were co-incubated with LD-CPD (50 μg mL−1) for 50 min. Confocal imaging was then performed in PBS. Intracellular fluorescence imaging was performed with blue channel (430–495 nm) and green channel (505–570 nm) excited at 405 nm.

Amphiphilic molecules have been confirmed to have great potential for lipid droplet-specific targeting ability [28]. In this work, using 1,6-dihydroxy naphthalene and ethylenediamine as precursors and conducting the reaction. Using the emission intensity as a criterion, the temperature and the amount of ethylenediamine used for the preparation of LD-CPDs were optimized. Considering that DHN is first oxidized to naphthoquinone under alkaline condition before the subsequent Schiff base reaction, an excess of ethylenediamine is more favorable for the preparation of LD-CPD. Under optimized synthetic conditions (Fig. S1), amphiphilic LD-CPD can be easily obtained (Scheme 1). Ethylenediamine was chosen as a good nucleophile to facilitate the Schiff-base reaction with 1,6-dihydroxy naphthalene. The amine group acts as a hydrophilic component, while the naphthalene core serves as a lipophilic group. By integrating covalent and self-assembly strategies, the oligomer efficiently forms nanoparticles. Ultimately, under continuous low-temperature conditions, LDs-targeted carbonized polymer dots are formed and named LD-CPD.

The morphology of the prepared LD-CPD was first investigated. As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, LD-CPD is a uniformly spherical structure with an average diameter of ∼3.3 nm and evenly dispersed in DMSO solution. The zeta potential of LD-CPD was determined to be −14.34 mV. The HRTEM illustration in Fig. 1A shows that the lattice spacing of LD-CPD is 0.31 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite-carbon [29], indicating that LD-CPD has a graphite-like structural core. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) results of LD-CPD show an obvious broad diffraction peak at about 24° (Fig. 1C), confirming the turbostratic carbon structure of LD-CPD. In addition, the chemical composition and surface functional groups of LD-CPD were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Obviously, it can be found in Fig. 1D that the prepared LD-CPD consists of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) reveals three carbon forms: C–C/CC (284.65 eV), C–N/C–O (285.25 eV) and NC/CO (288.2 eV). The N1s spectrum (Fig. S2A) shows two nitrogen species: aromatic N at 399.3 eV and amino N at 400.4 eV. The O1s spectrum (Fig. S2B) indicates three forms of oxygen belonging to CO (531.3 eV), C–O–C (532.2 eV) and C–OH (532.75 eV), respectively. FTIR analysis (Fig. 1F) yields similar findings, with observed stretching vibration at 3417 cm−1 for N–H/O–H and 1623 cm−1 for CN. Peaks at 1461 cm−1 and 1366 cm−1 are attributed to CC and C–N bonds, respectively, while those at 1579 cm−1 and 1249 cm−1 correspond to N–H and C–O bonds. The characteristic peak of CN at 1623 cm−1 confirmed the existence of the Schiff base structure [30], which was synthesized by the oxidized hydroxyl group of 1, 6-dihydroxy naphthalene and ethylenediamine.

The optical properties of LD-CPD were also assessed. As shown in Fig. 2 B, the strong absorption band at 275 nm is assigned to the π-π∗ transition of the CC bond of aromatic conjugated structure [31,32]. The optimal excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of LD-CPD in DMSO solvent are 435 nm and 513 nm, respectively. Moreover, the LD-CPD shows stable emission as the excitation wavelengths are varied from 400 nm to 460 nm (Fig. S3), indicating a defining excitation-independent characteristic, which is attributed to the existence of a single, pure luminescence center in LD-CPD [33]. And, the minimal overlap between the absorption and emission spectra indicates that self-absorption effects are significantly reduced. Considering the low-polarity environment of LDs, the solvatochromic nature of LD-CPD was further studied. Surprisingly, we find that LD-CPD has remarkable solvent-chromic properties. As shown in Fig. 2A and C, upon solvent polarity increasing from dioxane to methanol, an obvious fluorescence color change from blue to yellowish-green is observed, along with the redshift of maximum emission wavelength from 476 nm to 546 nm, which shows significant solvent polarity dependence. A linear correlation between the maximum emission wavelength of LD-CPD (λem max) and the solvent polarity parameter (ET (30)) is obtained (Fig. 2D), further confirming the prominent solvatochromism. What's more, it can be seen from Fig. 2A that LD-CPD has almost no fluorescence emissions in water, which undoubtedly provides a key condition for cell wash-free imaging. To further verify the polarity sensitivity of LD-CPD, as shown in Table S1, using coumarin 153 in ethanol (Φ = 0.53) as the reference, the quantum yields (QYs) of LD-CPD in different solvents were 6.69 % (MeOH), 12.43 % (EtOH), 31.96 % (DMSO), 28.32 % (DMF), 26.73 % (ACN), 23.42 % (ACE) and 24.05 % (Diox), respectively. This significant disparity demonstrates that LD-CPD is sensitive to polarity, showcasing their potential for monitoring polarity alterations within biological microenvironments. In addition, the effect of viscosity, a characteristic of the microenvironment surrounding subcellular organelle lipid droplets, on LD-CPD was evaluated using a methanol-glycerol mixture with decreasing viscosity. As shown in Fig. S4, with progressive reductions in viscosity, no significant change in the fluorescence intensity was determined, confirming that LD-CPD is unresponsive to viscosity. Encouraged by this, the optical properties of LD-CPD in n-octanol were assessed in Fig. 2E, which simulate the dominant lipid environment in LDs. As expected, a very bright green fluorescence is observed in oily media with a quantum yield (QY) of 17.32 %. These results demonstrate that LD-CPD possesses excellent fluorescence intensity (up to 260 times) and luminescence properties in lipids compared to that in water. Therefore, LD-CPD has great promise for targeted imaging of lipid droplets. The effect of pH on LD-CPD emission spectra was further studied in phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) containing 50 % Diox. As shown in Fig. S5, with increasing pH from 5 to 9, the fluorescence intensity of LD-CPD only decreased by about 28.2 %. In particular, the maximum emission wavelengths in different pH environments were almost the same, suggesting that the linear relationship between their maximum emission wavelengths and solvent polarity is not affected by pH.

To verify the feasibility of LD-CPD as a polarity probe, the fluorescence responses were investigated with mixed solutions of different polarities. As shown in Fig. 2F, with increasing the water contents of water/dioxane mixed solvents from 0 to 50 %, a similar fluorescence color change from blue to yellowish-green is observed (the inset in Fig. 2F), accompanied by a redshift of emission wavelength (63 nm) and a gradual decrease of fluorescence intensity. Meanwhile, the maximum fluorescence intensity and maximum emission wavelength show a good linear correlation with the logarithm of water content in the water/dioxane mixed system, with correlation coefficients R2 of 0.9965 and 0.9808, respectively (Figs. S6C and D), indicating that LD -CPD has the potential to detect polarity with satisfying sensitivity.

In general, solvatochromic responses are primarily attributed to solvent dipole-dipole interaction and hydrogen bonding [34]. The above experimental results should be attributed to the enhancement of hydrogen bonding with the increase of water content, which enhanced the internal conversion (IC), vibration relaxation (VR) and other non-radiative transitions in the process of the excited state going back to the ground state, resulting in the weakening of radiative transition and the decrease of fluorescence intensity. Due to the enhancement of hydrogen bond in the excited state, the energy level of excited state decreases more than that of ground state, resulting in the reduction of the transition level from ground state to excited state, resulting in the redshift of emission spectrum [35–37]. In addition, to study the influence of solvent dipole-dipole interaction on solvatochromism, LD-CPD was successively dissolved in n-octanol, n-butanol, isopropyl alcohol, ethanol, methanol with the polarity gradually increasing, as shown in Fig. 2G. With the increase of polarity in alcohol solvents, the emission peak undergoes a redshift of approximately 20 nm. This is because upon excitation, the dipole moment of LD-CPD in the excited state is greater than in the ground state, and the stabilizing effect of a polar solvent on the excited state is more pronounced than on the ground state, leading to a larger energy loss for the excited state in the polar solvent than in the ground state, which manifests as a redshift of emission peak [38]. In summary, the above experiments show that the solvatochromic properties are influenced by both hydrogen bonding and dipole-dipole interactions in the solvent, with hydrogen bonding having a greater impact than the dipole moment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell colocalization and wash free imaging experiment
ce:section.ce:para: Before fluorescent imaging, the cytotoxicity of LD-CPD to HeLa cells was assessed using the classical MTT assay. As shown in Fig. S8, at a concentration of 100 μg mL−1, the cell survival rate remained at 88.01 % after 24 h. After 48 h of incubation within the working concentration range (50 μg mL−1), the survival rate could also be as high as 89.74 %, indicating that LD-CPD possesses good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Next, to enhance the imaging performance, the staining time and concentrations of LD-CPD were optimized for the cell experiments (Fig. S9 and Fig. S10). Considering that LD-CPD is almost non-fluorescent in water, the feasibility as a “wash free” staining probe was evaluated under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. S11, there is no significant difference between the wash and no-wash groups after LD-CPD was incubated for 50 min, indicating that LD-CPD can be well used for wash free imaging.

To assess the targeting feature of LD-CPD for lipid droplets in living cells, three commercial probes, including Nile Red (lipid droplet targeting), Lyso-Tracker Red and Mito-Tracker Red, were employed for the colocalization experiment. The adherent HeLa cells stained with LD-CPD (50 μg mL−1) were incubated with Nile Red (1 μM) for 10 min. As control groups, the LD-CPD-located HeLa cells were incubated with Lyso-Tracker Red fluorescent probe (65 nM) and Mito-Tracker Red (100 nM) for 30 min respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 , the LD-CPD exhibits bright green emission, which almost overlaps with Nile Red with a high Pearson's correlation coefficient (0.95). For comparison, the dotted red fluorescence of Lyso-Tracker Red and Mito-Tracker Red barely merges with the green emission of LD-CPD and Pearson's correlation coefficients are only 0.42 and 0.34, respectively. These results indicate that LD-CPD shows good targeting specificity for lipid droplets. Compared to the recently reported polarity probes (Table S2), LD-CPD shows great potential for simple and dual-mode imaging of LD, which is urgently needed in LD-related research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polarity changes in cells during starvation and oxidative stress
ce:section.ce:para: Benefiting from the favorable solvent discoloration effect, LD-CPD exhibits excellent solubility and strong blue emission in low-polarity hydrophobic media, suggesting that it can be used as a fluorescent probe to image lipid droplets. The feasibility of LD-CPD as a ratiometric imaging probe for detecting polarity fluctuation within LDs during various stimulations was investigated. With 405 nm excitation, the fluorescence signals of the green channel (505–570 nm) and the blue channel (430–495 nm) were collected, representing the high and low polarity regions of the cell, respectively. Firstly, the probe LD-CPD was used for ratiometric imaging of lipid droplet polarity changes during starvation-induced autophagy. HeLa cells were starved to induce autophagy by cultivating them in serum-free medium (serum-free 1640 medium) at different times. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the blue channel and the green channel represent the regions with low polarity and high polarity in the cell respectively. As increasing the starvation treatment time, the fluorescence color changes in the green channel are more pronounced than those in the blue channel with the ratio (G/B) channel highlighting this difference even more clearly. It is obvious that the fluorescence intensity change of green channel is significantly higher than that of blue channel (Fig. 4B). This indicates a progressive increase in the polarity of intracellular lipid droplets in this case, which is similar to previous research results [15,39], leading to the redshift of LD-CPD [40]. This is because cells in a state of starvation consume lipids to maintain intracellular energy supply and sustain normal physiological processes, which in turn alters the polarity of LDs. Furthermore, starvation also induces the shrinkage of the hydrophobic core of LDs, which is probably detected by LD-CPD as the increase in their polarity. Additionally, starvation has been observed to induce autophagy and oxidative stress in cells [41], which may potentially lead to lipid peroxidation, further inducing increased LD polarity. Therefore, LD-CPD can be used as a fluorescent indicator to monitor the variations of LD polarity in living cells.

After tracking the behavior of LDs after starvation treatment, LD-CPD was further used to image the variation of LDs during drug-induced oxidative stress. Oxidative stress can induce lipid peroxidation, leading to polarity changes in intracellular lipid tissues [42,43]. Here, we have also investigated the alterations in the polarity of LDs in HeLa cells when exposed to H2O2 with 1 h. As shown in Fig. 5 A, Compared with the control group, enhanced green-channel fluorescence in H2O2-treated cells, evident in the ratio channel, indicates that LDs are susceptible to rapid oxidation under oxidative stress, leading to increased polarity [44]. In addition, the histograms of mean fluorescence intensity of green channel and blue channel match well with the results mentioned above (Fig. 5B). Therefore, the above results prove that LD-CPD can monitor the polarity changes of intracellular LDs under starvation or oxidative stress, which indicate that LD-CPD has great potential to monitor the polarity changes of LDs in living cells.

In summary, we have facilely synthesized a carbonized polymer dot with high specificity for lipid droplets targeting under mild conditions. The amphiphilic probe, featuring an amine group and a naphthalene core, demonstrates significant targeting affinity, closely matching Nile Red with a high Pearson's correlation coefficient of 0.95. Moreover, it is sensitive to solvent polarity and the maximum fluorescence intensity and maximum emission wavelength show a good linear correlation with the logarithm of water content in mixed solvents. By virtue of its solvatochromic nature and graphite-like structural core, the prepared LD-CPD show significant polarity-sensitive emission shifts, strong photobleaching resistance, good biocompatibility and turn-off response to water, making them ideal for water-free imaging of LDs. Ratiometric imaging has confirmed the successful application of CPDs in monitoring subtle changes in intracellular polarity during starvation and oxidative stress.

Dongmei Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Genxiu Yao: Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Junyong Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Juanjuan Liang: Validation. Huan Zhou: Visualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 22304131), the Key Project of Anhui Provincial Department of Education Scientific Research Project (2023AH051750, 2023AH051749), Young Backbone Teacher In-Country Study and Research Project of Anhui Educational Committee(JNFX2023034) and Program for excellent Sci-tech Innovation Teams of Universities in Anhui Province (2023AH010073)
